params.text:
Three different things

People often confuse whistleblowing with two other routes: a complaint and a grievance. They overlap, but they are not the same, and each has its own process.

  • A whistleblowing concern is raised in the public interest — it is about wrongdoing or risk that could harm others.
  • A complaint is usually raised by a person who uses the service, or their family, about the quality of care or service they have personally received.
  • A grievance is a personal workplace issue that affects you — for example, a dispute about your pay, your rota or how a manager has treated you.

If you are unsure which route applies, do not let that stop you speaking up. Raise the concern and let it be directed to the right process.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Match each situation to the right route.

dialogs.text: A carer is rough when moving a resident and you fear they could be hurt.
dialogs.answer: Whistleblowing concern — it is about risk of harm to another person, raised in the public interest.

dialogs.text: A resident's daughter is unhappy that meals arrive late.
dialogs.answer: Complaint — it is about the quality of service a person has personally received.

dialogs.text: You feel your shift pattern is unfair compared with a colleague's.
dialogs.answer: Grievance — it is a personal workplace matter affecting you, not a risk to others.

dialogs.text: You suspect a manager is falsifying medication records to hide errors.
dialogs.answer: Whistleblowing concern — a cover-up of wrongdoing that puts people at risk.

dialogs.text: You believe you were unfairly passed over for a promotion.
dialogs.answer: Grievance — a personal employment issue, handled through the grievance procedure.
